narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at their academic records, family backgrounds, and other factors to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 20, lower-middle family income bracket, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 3, and your plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 46% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their legal careers. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and family income level. Most significantly, your LSAT score stood out as the top influence pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, especially since it's well below the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest influence but in the opposite direction, pulling the prediction toward bar passage success compared to many other students. Your lower-middle family income also helped somewhat, nudging the model slightly toward expecting you to pass, likely because students from similar backgrounds have shown resilience in the data. While your full-time enrollment plan aligns with the vast majority of students (over 90%), and your early school deciles were below average, they played smaller roles here. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having solid intent to study full-time but lagging in key metrics like LSAT and grades that strongly correlate with bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your determination as an Asian male overcoming these hurdles. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on these areas, and with your full-time commitment, you're well-positioned to do the same.
